props.content: Tip vs. Fare Amount — Correlation, Scatter & Predictive Model

There is a **moderate positive correlation** (Pearson r = 0.52, Spearman r = 0.39) between fare and tip. The linear model explains ~27% of tip variance — fare is a meaningful but not dominant predictor. Payment type (cash vs. credit card) is a major confounding factor.
Scatter Plot: Tip vs. Fare (colored by Payment Type, with Regression Line)

props.title: Tip Amount vs. Fare Amount by Payment Type
Regression Line: Predicted Tip vs. Fare

props.content: Use the **Payment Type** filter above to isolate credit card vs. cash trips. Cash rides show $0 tip (recorded), while credit card rides average $2.17 — filtering reveals the true correlation within each segment.

Tips scale consistently with fare: from **$0.41** on sub-$5 fares to **$3.78** on $30+ fares. This monotonic increase validates the linear model's direction, though the relationship is not perfectly linear.
Avg Tip & Fare by Payment Type

props.title: Avg Tip & Avg Fare by Payment Type
Cash Rides Suppress Correlation
props.content: 145 cash trips (29%) record $0 tip, dragging down the overall correlation. Among credit card rides only, the correlation is substantially stronger. The model should ideally be trained on credit card trips separately.

props.caption: Linear regression predictions — tip converges toward ~12% of fare at higher amounts

props.content: The model predicts **~$0.12 tip per $1 of fare**. At higher fares, the tip percentage stabilizes near 12%. The intercept ($0.09) is negligible, suggesting tipping is nearly proportional to fare for credit card riders.
